file_name: Garg and Jindal - 2023 - FDLM An enhanced feature based deep learning model for skin lesion detection.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an enhanced feature-based deep learning model (FDLM) for skin lesion detection. Specifically, it employs an improved LeNet architecture (a type of Convolutional Neural Network) trained with features optimized by the cuckoo search technique. The model uses hybrid shape descriptors (SURF and ORB) to classify skin lesions as benign or malignant, achieving an accuracy of 99.62%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original experimental study proposing a new deep learning model (FDLM) for skin lesion detection. It details the development of an improved LeNet architecture, feature optimization using cuckoo search, and hybrid shape descriptors (SURF and ORB). The authors report original results, including a 99.62% accuracy on the ISIC dataset, and compare their method against existing techniques, which characterizes it as a research paper rather than a review.

title: FDLM: An enhanced feature based deep learning model for skin lesion detection
brief_summary: This study proposes FDLM, an enhanced feature-based deep learning model for the automated detection of skin lesions to distinguish between benign and malignant cases. The method utilizes an improved LeNet architecture trained with a feature set optimized by the cuckoo search technique, incorporating hybrid shape descriptors such as SURF and ORB. The model was evaluated using a dataset from a centralized repository, specifically the ISIC dataset, to assess its classification performance. The primary evaluation metric was accuracy, comparing the proposed hybrid feature combinations against other distinct feature sets and existing methods. The results demonstrated that the CNN with hybrid SURF and ORB features achieved the highest accuracy of 99.62%, outperforming other combinations and previous state-of-the-art techniques. The findings confirm the effectiveness of using optimized hybrid features with deep learning for accurate skin lesion detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study performs a two-class classification to distinguish between benign and malignant skin lesions. The term 'benign cancer' is used in the text to describe contained lesions, though clinically these are benign neoplasms or lesions.

The study performs a two-class classification to distinguish between benign and malignant skin lesions. The text broadly refers to the target as 'skin cancer' or 'malignant' lesions, without specifying subtypes like melanoma or BCC in the experimental labels.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (benign vs. malignant skin lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states it uses a two-class classification approach to determine if a skin lesion is cancerous (malignant) or non-cancerous (benign). The model predicts discrete classes using a CNN with a softmax layer for probability determination of each class. The primary evaluation metric is accuracy (99.62%).

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: A dataset from a centralized repository used for skin lesion detection. The paper explicitly mentions using the ISIC dataset for evaluation in the abstract and summary, though specific details like the year (e.g., ISIC 2017, 2018) or source URL are not provided in the text.

use.training: Used for training the FDLM model (implied by the methodology of training the CNN with optimized features).
use.validation: Used for validation (implied by standard deep learning practices and comparison with other works, but specific split not reported).
use.testing: Used for testing the model's performance, achieving 99.62% accuracy.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy metrics and compared against existing methods without statistical significance testing.

ISIC dataset.notes: The proposed model achieved a maximum performance of 99.62% at Level-2 hybrid features in the abstract and conclusion, but Table 3 and Section 10 report the final comparison accuracy as 99.98%. The dataset consisted of 1326 images (663 benign, 663 malignant), with 60% used for training and 40% for testing. Other feature combinations at Level-1 and Level-2 showed accuracies between 95-99.6% and 98-99.6% respectively, with performance degrading at Levels 3 and 4.

architecture_used.main_model: FDLM (Feature-based Deep Learning Model) based on an improved LeNet architecture

architecture_details.backbone: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on LeNet with modifications
architecture_details.novelty: Input consists of optimized hybrid shape descriptors (SURF and ORB) extracted via FIFE and Cuckoo Search optimization, rather than raw image pixels

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Abuzaghleh et al. [18] (automated skin lesion segmentation and analysis based on color and shape)
Kavitha et al. [19] (feature-based method using GLCM and SURF with SVM and KNN classifiers)
Nasir et al. [20] (hybrid method with SVM classifier)
Khan et al. [21] (multi-class support vector machine)
Upadhyay and Chandra [22] (Support Vector Machine with bag of visual words)
Mahbod et al. [23] (AlexNet, VGG16, ResNet-18 with Support Vector Machine classifiers)
Seeja and Suresh [24] (CNN based U-net algorithm with K-Nearest Neighbor, Random Forest, Naive Bayes, and Support Vector Machine classifiers)
Javed et al. [25] (Support Vector Machine with deep color and PHOG-optimized features)
Bansal et al. [26] (SVM classifier with texture and geometric elements)
preprocessing_techniques: Feature extraction using hybrid shape descriptors (SURF and ORB)
Feature optimization using Cuckoo Search technique
Feature reduction/selection to minimize computation time and memory
Data splitting: 60% for training, 40% for testing
conclusions: The proposed FDLM model, which utilizes an improved LeNet architecture trained with hybrid SURF and ORB features optimized by the cuckoo search technique, achieved the highest accuracy of 99.98% for skin lesion detection.
The study demonstrates that combining hybrid shape descriptors (specifically at Level-2) improves classification performance compared to single descriptors or higher-level combinations.
Performance degrades when feature combination levels exceed two, indicating that combining features up to Level-2 is sufficient for optimal performance.

The proposed method outperforms existing state-of-the-art techniques, including those by Abuzaghleh et al. (97%), Nasir et al. (97.50%), Khan et al. (97.75%), Seeja and Suresh (85.19%), Javed et al. (98.80%), and Bansal et al. (89.40%).
Feature reduction using the cuckoo search optimization technique effectively minimizes computation time and memory usage while maintaining high accuracy.
The findings confirm the usefulness of optimized hybrid features combined with deep learning models for accurate and efficient skin lesion detection.
important_remarks: The study uses a very small dataset of only 1,326 images (663 benign, 663 malignant), which limits the generalizability and robustness of the deep learning model.
The paper does not specify the exact version or year of the ISIC dataset used, nor does it provide a detailed breakdown of the data split beyond a 60/40 training/testing ratio.
There is a discrepancy in reported accuracy: the abstract and conclusion state 99.62%, while Table 3 and Section 10 report 99.98% for the proposed method.
The paper lacks statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) to validate the superiority of the proposed method over existing techniques.

The model relies on hand-crafted hybrid shape descriptors (SURF and ORB) optimized by Cuckoo Search rather than end-to-end deep learning feature extraction, which may limit its adaptability to diverse image qualities compared to modern CNNs.

The study is limited to a binary classification task (benign vs. malignant) and does not address multi-class classification for specific skin cancer subtypes (e.g., melanoma vs. basal cell carcinoma).
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on a single centralized repository dataset, raising concerns about potential dataset bias.

The paper does not report sensitivity, specificity, precision, or F1-score, relying solely on accuracy, which can be misleading if class distributions are imbalanced or if the threshold is not optimized.
The authors acknowledge that combining more than two feature descriptors can degrade performance, suggesting that the feature engineering process is sensitive to complexity.
The study claims ethical approval but provides no details on patient consent or data anonymization procedures for the centralized repository data.
